title: Python (programming language)
text: Python is a high-level, general-purpose programming language created by Guido van Rossum and first released in 1991. It emphasizes code readability with significant indentation and supports procedural, object-oriented, and functional programming. Python is widely used in data science, web development, and automation.

title: Rust (programming language)
text: Rust is a systems programming language focused on memory safety and performance. It guarantees memory safety without a garbage collector by using an ownership model with a borrow checker enforced at compile time. Rust was originally sponsored by Mozilla and first appeared in 2010.

title: JavaScript
text: JavaScript is a programming language that runs in web browsers and enables interactive web pages. It was created by Brendan Eich in 1995 and conforms to the ECMAScript specification. With the Node.js runtime, JavaScript can also run on servers outside the browser.

title: SQL and relational databases
text: SQL, or Structured Query Language, is used to manage and query data in relational databases. It provides statements such as SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE to read and modify tables made of rows and columns. Relational databases organize data into tables linked by keys.

title: Git version control
text: Git is a distributed version control system created by Linus Torvalds in 2005 to manage development of the Linux kernel. It tracks changes to files over time, allowing many developers to collaborate using branches and commits. Each clone of a Git repository contains the full history of the project.

title: HTTP protocol
text: The Hypertext Transfer Protocol, or HTTP, is the foundation of data communication on the World Wide Web. Clients send requests using methods such as GET and POST, and servers return responses with status codes like 200 for success and 404 for not found. HTTPS adds encryption using TLS to secure the connection.

title: TCP/IP networking
text: TCP/IP is the suite of communication protocols used to interconnect network devices on the internet. The Transmission Control Protocol provides reliable, ordered delivery of data, while the Internet Protocol handles addressing and routing of packets. Together they enable end-to-end communication across networks.

title: Photosynthesis
text: Photosynthesis is the process by which green plants, algae, and some bacteria convert light energy into chemical energy. Using chlorophyll, they transform carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen in the presence of sunlight. This process is the basis of most food chains on Earth.

title: Mitochondria
text: Mitochondria are organelles found in most eukaryotic cells and are often called the powerhouse of the cell. They generate most of the cell's supply of adenosine triphosphate, or ATP, through cellular respiration. Mitochondria contain their own small genome separate from the nucleus.

title: DNA
text: Deoxyribonucleic acid, or DNA, carries the genetic instructions used in the growth and functioning of living organisms. Its structure is a double helix composed of nucleotide bases adenine, thymine, guanine, and cytosine. The double-helix model was described by James Watson and Francis Crick in 1953.

title: Newton's laws of motion
text: Isaac Newton's three laws of motion form the foundation of classical mechanics. The first law states that an object remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted on by a force, and the second relates force to mass times acceleration. The third law states that for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction.

title: Theory of relativity
text: Albert Einstein's theory of relativity transformed the understanding of space, time, and gravity. Special relativity, published in 1905, showed that the speed of light is constant and that time and space are relative to the observer. General relativity, published in 1915, describes gravity as the curvature of spacetime caused by mass.

title: Black holes
text: A black hole is a region of spacetime where gravity is so strong that nothing, not even light, can escape. Black holes form when massive stars collapse at the end of their life cycles. The boundary beyond which escape is impossible is called the event horizon.

title: The Sun
text: The Sun is the star at the center of the solar system and the primary source of energy for life on Earth. It is a nearly perfect sphere of hot plasma that generates energy through nuclear fusion of hydrogen into helium in its core. The Sun accounts for about 99.8 percent of the total mass of the solar system.

title: Mars
text: Mars is the fourth planet from the Sun and is often called the Red Planet because of iron oxide on its surface. It has the largest volcano in the solar system, Olympus Mons, and two small moons named Phobos and Deimos. Mars has a thin atmosphere composed mostly of carbon dioxide.

title: Jupiter
text: Jupiter is the largest planet in the solar system, a gas giant composed mainly of hydrogen and helium. Its most famous feature is the Great Red Spot, a giant storm that has raged for centuries. Jupiter has dozens of moons, including the four large Galilean moons discovered in 1610.

title: The Moon
text: The Moon is Earth's only natural satellite and the fifth largest moon in the solar system. Its gravitational pull is the main cause of the ocean tides on Earth. The Moon always shows the same face to Earth because its rotation period equals its orbital period.

title: The water cycle
text: The water cycle describes the continuous movement of water on, above, and below the surface of the Earth. Water evaporates from oceans and lakes, condenses into clouds, and falls back as precipitation such as rain or snow. This cycle distributes fresh water across the planet.

title: Plate tectonics
text: Plate tectonics is the theory that Earth's outer shell is divided into large plates that move over the mantle. The interactions of these plates cause earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, and the formation of mountain ranges. The theory was widely accepted in the 1960s and built on earlier ideas of continental drift.

title: The French Revolution
text: The French Revolution began in 1789 and brought an end to the absolute monarchy in France. It was driven by widespread discontent with inequality, financial crisis, and Enlightenment ideas about liberty and equality. The revolution led to the rise of Napoleon Bonaparte and reshaped modern European politics.

title: World War II
text: World War II was a global conflict that lasted from 1939 to 1945 and involved most of the world's nations. It was fought between the Allied powers and the Axis powers led by Germany, Italy, and Japan. The war ended in 1945 after the surrender of Germany and then Japan.

title: The Roman Empire
text: The Roman Empire was one of the largest and most influential civilizations in history, centered on the city of Rome. At its height under emperors such as Trajan, it spanned much of Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East. It is remembered for its engineering, law, and the spread of Latin.

title: The printing press
text: The printing press with movable metal type was introduced to Europe by Johannes Gutenberg around 1440. It dramatically lowered the cost of producing books and accelerated the spread of knowledge during the Renaissance. The Gutenberg Bible was among the first major books printed using this method.

title: Caffeine
text: Caffeine is a natural stimulant found in coffee, tea, and cacao plants. It works by blocking the action of adenosine, a chemical that promotes sleep, which helps people feel more alert. Caffeine is the most widely consumed psychoactive substance in the world.

title: Vaccines
text: Vaccines train the immune system to recognize and fight specific pathogens without causing the disease. They typically contain a weakened or inactivated microbe, or instructions for making a harmless piece of it, prompting the body to produce antibodies. Widespread vaccination has eradicated or controlled many infectious diseases.

title: Ohm's law
text: Ohm's law states that the electric current through a conductor is directly proportional to the voltage across it and inversely proportional to its resistance. It is commonly written as V equals I times R, where V is voltage, I is current, and R is resistance. The law is fundamental to analyzing electrical circuits.

title: The Great Barrier Reef
text: The Great Barrier Reef is the world's largest coral reef system, located off the coast of Queensland, Australia. It stretches over 2,300 kilometers and is composed of thousands of individual reefs and hundreds of islands. The reef hosts a vast diversity of marine life but is threatened by coral bleaching.

title: Mount Everest
text: Mount Everest is the highest mountain on Earth above sea level, located in the Himalayas on the border between Nepal and China. Its peak reaches about 8,849 meters above sea level. The first confirmed ascent was achieved by Edmund Hillary and Tenzing Norgay in 1953.
